what_it_does: Defines an HTML render-proof page for N-panel decision cards, loading shared styles and runtime scripts, fetching layout and decision data, and mounting three explained decision-card examples with different grounding behaviors.
observations: The page creates two initial full-height mount regions, m1 and m2, then adds m3 dynamically for a binary-branch case.
It uses the decision-card archetype and renders each record through DNA.renderArchetype followed by DNA.renderExplained.
The examples cover a synchronous grounded string, an asynchronous structured explanation object, and a synchronous binary decision case.
Record IDs are hard-coded: substrate-home, rerank-loadout, and merge-sa-authorize.
The async example resolves after five seconds and is labeled as a placeholder-to-stream test in the inline comment.
standouts: Acts as a focused visual proof page for explained decision rendering behavior.
Exercises both string and structured explanation payloads.
Includes a two-option branch case through the dynamically added m3 mount.
conventions: Keeps the proof self-contained in one HTML file with inline setup script.
Uses shared surface CSS and runtime scripts instead of defining local rendering logic from scratch.
Uses hard-coded fixture IDs and mock grounded resolve functions for deterministic render cases.
Breaks the initial static mount pattern by adding the third mount region from JavaScript.
concerns: The five-second async delay may make the proof feel stalled unless the placeholder behavior is the subject being checked.
Hard-coded record IDs mean missing content records produce only inline text rather than a stronger test failure signal.
m3 styling is applied imperatively and does not include the same border-bottom rule as m1 and m2.
notes: A small render-proof page that mounts three decision-card explanation scenarios against live layout and decision fixtures.
questions: Should m3 intentionally differ from m1 and m2 by not having a bottom border?
Is the five-second async delay chosen to represent streaming latency or just to make the placeholder state visible?
Should the subtype theorem-fork apply only to the async rerank-loadout case?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated render-proof purpose matches what the file does: it mounts decision-card examples to exercise sync, async structured, and binary explanation regions.
intention_signals: It seems intended to verify that the decision-card explanation layer renders grounded content correctly across multiple payload shapes and panel arrangements.
novelty: The file combines archetype rendering with mocked explanation resolution so the same proof page can show immediate text, delayed structured content, and binary-panel placement.
contribution: Contributes a compact fixture-backed proof surface for retaining decision explanation rendering cases, including sync text, async structured explanation data, and binary decision layout behavior.
summary_for_embedding: HTML render-proof page that fetches layout and decision fixtures and mounts three explained decision-card examples covering synchronous, asynchronous structured, and binary rendering cases.
intention_for_embedding: Created to check how the N-panel decision explanation layer behaves under different grounded-resolution shapes and panel arrangements.
